g.text: You're pregnant. From this point forward, your body will do
something extraordinary — growing a new life from a single
cell. Some days you'll feel amazing. Others, you'll wonder
what happened to your energy, your appetite, or your ability
All of it is normal. This guide walks you through what to
expect, what to do about it, and what decisions are ahead —
Every pregnancy is different. This is not a substitute for
your provider's care — it's a companion to help you know
what to ask and what to expect.
Your body is building a placenta, increasing blood volume,
and supporting rapid cell division. That takes enormous
Rest when you can — don't fight it. Naps aren't laziness,
Rising hormone levels affect your digestive system. It can
hit any time of day, not just mornings.
Dry crackers before getting up. Small bites every 30
min. Ginger tea or chews. Prenatal vitamin at bedtime.
Hormonal changes can make your breasts swollen and
A well-fitted, supportive bra makes a real difference.
Your digestive system slows down to absorb more
Increase fluids, fresh fruits, and vegetables. Chewable
papaya enzyme tablets after meals can ease gas.
Mild, menstrual-like cramps are normal as your uterus
Sharp or severe pain is not normal — call your provider.
, early eye structures, and limb buds
— Blood type, Rh status, infection screening, and baseline health markers.
Discuss genetic testing with your provider
— Several options exist at different stages. Understand what's available, what insurance covers, and what the results can and can't tell you.
— About 1 in 10 babies are born early. Know your risk factors, the warning signs, and the plan if it happens.
— High blood pressure in pregnancy can lead to pre-eclampsia. Early monitoring makes a difference.
— Review what's covered for prenatal care, delivery, and newborn care before you need it.
— Your body is working harder than it feels. Sleep when you're tired. It's not laziness — it's biology.
— Even 10 minutes of walking a day helps with fatigue, mood, and sleep quality.
— Your baby's umbilical cord contains stem cells used in transplant medicine for over 30 years. You'll need to decide before delivery. See Panel 14.
Better sleep, returning appetite, and a noticeable lift in
how you feel. This is often the most comfortable stretch of
Take advantage of this window — sign up for classes,
prepare the nursery, enjoy how you feel.
Most women start feeling movement around week 23 —
beginning as flutters and building to distinct kicks and rolls.
One of the most exciting milestones. By week 28, you'll
Increased blood volume and uterine pressure can cause
veins in your legs to swell.
Wear support stockings. Elevate your legs when
Mild uterine tightening 2–4 times a day is your body
practicing for labor. Normal and not dangerous.
If contractions come 3–4 times in one hour, call your
If diagnosed, your iron levels need support. This is common
Eat more meat, leafy greens, and beans. Take iron with
juice, not milk. Increase fiber to offset constipation.
are getting longer. Fingers open and close.
Covered in fine hair called lanugo.
— a substance essential for breathing air.
— A blood test between weeks 15–20 that screens for genetic disorders and neural tube defects. Most accurate at 16–18 weeks.
— Between weeks 18–22. A detailed evaluation of your baby's anatomy — brain, heart, limbs, and kidneys.
— Beginning at week 20. Right or left side is fine — the goal is staying off your back. A body pillow can help.
— Between weeks 24–28. A one-hour blood test that screens for gestational diabetes.
— At week 28 if you are Rh-negative. This protects your current and future pregnancies.
— Starting at week 28. When resting, you should feel at least 4 movements in one hour. Talk to your provider about their specific guidelines.
— Partner, family member, friend, or doula. Think about who you want in the room.
Sign up for childbirth and newborn care classes
— Prepared childbirth, breastfeeding, and infant CPR. These fill up — register early.
— Your baby's umbilical cord blood contains stem cells that have been used to treat over 80 serious conditions. It's worth understanding your options before delivery. See Panel 14.
Bleeding, leaking fluid, or changes in vision
Pressure or pain 3–4 times per hour
Your baby pushes your stomach upward, causing acid
reflux after meals or when lying flat.
Smaller meals, more often. Stay upright 30 min after
eating. Lie on your right side. Antacids are safe.
Digestive slowdown and pelvic pressure make both
More fruits, vegetables, and water. Ask about a stool
softener. OTC hemorrhoid remedies are available.
Extra weight shifts your center of gravity. Cramps tend to
Bend at knees, not waist. For cramps, point toes toward
belly and try ice. Prenatal massage and PT can help.
Fluid retention causes swollen feet/ankles. Bladder
pressure can cause leaking when you laugh or cough.
Elevate legs. Increase water and protein. Kegels (50–
100/day) help with leaking. Sudden severe swelling —
Interrupted sleep takes a toll on energy and emotions.
If you feel overwhelmed, anxious, or persistently sad,
talk to your provider. Common and treatable.
Sleep and wake cycles are developing. Hiccups are a
they'll likely stay in until delivery.
— A vaginal and rectal swab between weeks 35–37. If positive, you'll receive antibiotics during labor to protect your baby.
— Interview candidates and confirm they accept your insurance. Do this before your due date.
— Know the facility, where to park, and the fastest route from home.
— You will not be allowed to leave the hospital without one properly installed.
— For you and for baby. Include your insurance card, ID, phone charger, and going-home outfits.
— A panel of tests performed at birth that screens your baby for a range of serious but treatable conditions.
— Discuss pain management, labor positions, and who will be in the room with your provider and support team.
— If you haven't enrolled yet, it's not too late — even days before delivery. Visit
